questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a CSS comment?
questions.explanation: CSS uses /* */ for comments. // works in Sass/Less but not in vanilla CSS.

questions.question: Can CSS comments be nested like /* /* */ */?
options: No, the inner */ closes the outer comment
Yes, but only in modern browsers
questions.explanation: CSS does not support nested comments. The first */ encountered ends the comment that was opened with /*.

questions.question: Which of these is a GOOD use of a CSS comment?
options: p { color: blue; /* makes text blue */ }
/* Using !important here to override third-party widget styles */
questions.explanation: Good comments explain WHY something was done (like overriding third-party styles). Don't state the obvious.

questions.question: How do you temporarily disable a CSS rule using comments?
questions.explanation: Wrap the CSS rule in /* */ to comment it out. The browser will ignore the commented code.

questions.question: What happens to CSS comments when the page renders?
options: They appear in the page source but not the visual output
They show as green text on the page
questions.explanation: Comments appear in the HTML source/CSS file but have no effect on the visual rendering of the page.

questions.question: Can you use // comments in vanilla CSS?
options: No, // is only valid in CSS preprocessors like Sass
No, // comments cause a syntax error
questions.explanation: Single-line // comments are NOT valid in vanilla CSS. They work in Sass, Less, and other preprocessors.

questions.question: What is the best practice for commenting CSS?
questions.explanation: Explain WHY you made certain decisions (browser hacks, fallbacks, design choices). The CSS code itself shows WHAT it does.

questions.question: Should CSS comments be kept in production files?
options: No, they should be removed/minified for production
Only TODO comments should be kept
questions.explanation: Comments increase file size. Use CSS minification tools to strip comments in production.

questions.question: What does TODO mean in a CSS comment?
options: A task that still needs to be done
questions.explanation: TODO marks something that still needs to be addressed, like adding a fallback or implementing a feature.

questions.question: Can comments appear inside CSS property values?
options: Yes, but only in values with spaces
Yes, but only in modern CSS
questions.explanation: Comments cannot appear inside property values. They can appear between declarations or around entire rules.

questions.question: What happens when you use <!-- --> inside a CSS stylesheet?
options: It hides the CSS from older browsers
questions.explanation: HTML comments <!-- --> are not valid in CSS and may cause parsing errors. CSS only recognizes /* */ for comments.

questions.question: Which comment style is preserved in compiled Sass output?
questions.explanation: In Sass, /* */ comments are preserved in compiled CSS output, while // comments are stripped during compilation.

questions.question: What is a FIXME comment used for?
options: Flagging a known bug that needs fixing
questions.explanation: FIXME flags areas with known bugs or issues that need to be addressed, often with a short description of the problem.

questions.question: What is a documentation-style comment block in CSS?
questions.explanation: Documentation blocks use /** ... */ with structured fields describing the component's usage, modifiers, and states.

questions.question: How should section headers be formatted in CSS comments?
questions.explanation: Section headers like /* ===== SECTION NAME ===== */ create visually distinct markers that help navigate long stylesheets.

questions.question: Why is it important to update comments when code changes?
options: Comments affect how the CSS renders
Stale comments are misleading and erode developer trust
The browser validates comments against code
questions.explanation: Stale comments that contradict the actual code are worse than no comments because they actively mislead developers.

questions.question: What is the risk of leaving commented-out code in production?
options: It increases page load time significantly
It creates dead code that adds maintenance burden
questions.explanation: Commented-out code clutters the stylesheet and creates maintenance burden. Use version control history instead of commented-out code.

questions.question: Which CSS comment annotation is commonly used for browser-specific hacks?
questions.explanation: HACK annotations explain why a browser-specific workaround is needed, making the intent clear to other developers.

questions.question: What does date-stamping a CSS comment accomplish?
options: Makes the comment visible at a specific date
Provides traceability for when a decision was made
Causes the comment to expire automatically
Triggers a build tool to remove it
questions.explanation: Date-stamped comments (e.g., /* 2024-01-15: ... */) provide traceability and context for when and why certain decisions were made.

questions.question: Which tool can automatically remove CSS comments in production?
questions.explanation: CSS minification tools like cssnano, CleanCSS, and UglifyCSS automatically strip comments during the build process for production.
